Appendix B: Full Analysis Code 
#### Setup ####
library(tidyverse)
library(Amelia) #To visualize missingness
library(gt)       #fancy tables
library(gtExtras) #fancier tables.
library(scales)

# --- Load all tables ---
student_info         <- read_csv("studentInfo.csv")
student_registration <- read_csv("studentRegistration.csv")
student_assessment   <- read_csv("studentAssessment.csv")
student_vle          <- read_csv("studentVle.csv")
assessments          <- read_csv("assessments.csv")
courses              <- read_csv("courses.csv")
vle                  <- read_csv("vle.csv")


#>The Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (OULAD) is a publicly available 
#>dataset containing anonymized student data from the UK's Open University, 
#>covering 22 module presentations across 7 courses from 2013 and 2014. It 
#>includes demographic information, assessment results, and detailed Virtual 
#>Learning Environment interaction logs for 32,593 students,

#_______________________________________________________________________________
#_______________________________________________________________________________
# OULAD VARIABLE REFERENCE
#_______________________________________________________________________________

# --- studentInfo ---
# One row per student per module-presentation. Central table of the dataset —
# contains student demographics and their final result for each module taken.
# code_module          — module identifier. Real module names were replaced with
#                        letter codes (AAA, BBB, CCC, DDD, EEE,
#                        FFF, GGG) to preserve anonymity. AAA, BBB and GGG are
#                        Social Sciences modules; CCC, DDD, EEE and FFF are STEM.
# code_presentation    — identifies when the module ran. The letter suffix
#                        indicates the start month — J = October, B = February.
#                        The number is the year. So "2013J" started October 2013
#                        and "2014B" started February 2014.
# id_student           — unique student ID (randomized during anonymization —
#                        does not correspond to any real OU student number)
# gender               — student gender
# region               — UK region where student lived during the module
# highest_education    — highest qualification on entry
# imd_band             — Index of Multiple Deprivation band of student's home
#                        area. A measure of socioeconomic disadvantage based on
#                        postcode — lower band = more deprived area.
# age_band             — banded age group (not exact age — generalized during
#                        anonymization to protect student identity)
# num_of_prev_attempts — how many times student previously attempted this module
# studied_credits      — total credits student is studying concurrently
# disability           — whether student declared a disability
# final_result         — Pass / Fail / Distinction / Withdrawn
#____________________________


# --- studentRegistration ---
# One row per student per module-presentation. Records when the student
# signed up and, if applicable, when they dropped out.
# date_registration    — day student registered, expressed as days relative to
#                        the module start date (negative = registered early)
# date_unregistration  — day student unregistered (empty if they completed)
#____________________________


# --- studentAssessment ---
# One row per student per assessment submitted. Records the score and
# submission date for each individual assessment a student handed in.
# id_assessment        — links to assessments table
# date_submitted       — day assessment was submitted, relative to module start
# is_banked            — result carried over from a previous presentation
#                        (student gets credit without resubmitting)
# score                — 0-100, below 40 is a Fail
#____________________________


# --- assessments ---
# One row per assessment. The catalog of all assessments across all modules
# and presentations — metadata about what each assessment is, not who took it.
# id_assessment        — unique assessment identifier
# assessment_type      — TMA (Tutor Marked Assessment — marked by a human tutor),
#                        CMA (Computer Marked Assessment — auto-graded), or
#                        Exam (final exam at end of module)
# date                 — assessment cut-off day, relative to module start
# weight               — percentage weight of this assessment. Exams are treated
#                        separately at 100%; all other assessments sum to 100%.
#____________________________


# --- courses ---
# One row per module-presentation. Contains structural information about
# each module offering — primarily its length.
# length               — module duration in days from start date to end date
#____________________________

# --- studentVle ---
# One row per student per material per day. The raw interaction log —
# 10 million rows recording every click a student made in the VLE.
# id_site              — links to vle table (identifies the specific material)
# date                 — day of interaction, relative to module start
# sum_click            — number of clicks on that material that day
#____________________________


# --- vle ---
# One row per VLE material. The catalog of all learning materials available
# across all modules — metadata about what each material is, not who used it.
# id_site              — unique material identifier
# activity_type        — type of VLE material (e.g. resource, quiz, forum,
#                        oucontent, url, page, subpage, glossary, etc.)
# week_from            — week material is planned to become available
# week_to              — week material is planned to stop being used
#_______________________________________________________________________________


#_______________________________________________________________________________
# VARIABLES CREATED
#_______________________________________________________________________________

# --- domain ---
# Derived from code_module. Groups the 7 anonymized module codes into their
# subject area as documented in the OULAD paper.
# Social Sciences — AAA, BBB, GGG
# STEM            — CCC, DDD, EEE, FFF

# --- wf ---
# Withdrawal and Failure flag. Collapses final_result into a binary outcome
# for non-completion analysis. D grades do not exist in the OU system so
# DWF reduces to WF here.
# WF         — Withdrawn or Fail (did not successfully complete)
# Successful — Pass or Distinction (successfully completed)

# --- avg_score ---
# Mean assessment score across all submitted assessments for the student
# in that module. Excludes missing scores (na.rm = TRUE).

# --- min_score ---
# Lowest individual assessment score submitted by the student in that module.

# --- max_score ---
# Highest individual assessment score submitted by the student in that module.

# --- n_assessments ---
# Count of assessments with a recorded score. Uses sum(!is.na(score)) rather
# than n() to avoid inflating the count with missing exam scores.

# --- n_failed ---
# Count of assessments where score < 40. Per OULAD documentation, a score
# below 40 is interpreted as a Fail.

# --- avg_days_early ---
# Mean difference between assessment due date and submission date across all
# assessments. Positive = submitted early, negative = submitted late.

# --- total_clicks ---
# Total number of VLE clicks recorded for the student across the whole module.
# Students with no VLE interactions have NA here (3,365 students).

# --- total_days_active ---
# Number of distinct days the student interacted with the VLE.
# A measure of engagement consistency rather than volume.

# --- module_presentation_length ---
# Length of the module in days from start to end date, from the courses table.
#_______________________________________________________________________________








#### Pre-Join Inspection ####
#_______________________________________________________________________________
# PRE-JOIN DATA INSPECTION
# Run these before joining to verify column names, key values and row counts
# so we can confirm the joins will behave as expected.
#_______________________________________________________________________________

# --- Row counts ---
# Tells us the size of each table before any joining
nrow(student_info)
nrow(student_registration)
nrow(student_assessment) #number of student assessment submissions
nrow(assessments) #number of total assessments
nrow(courses)
nrow(student_vle)#The click events that exists in the VLE.
nrow(vle) #The material that exists in the VLE.

# --- Column names ---
# Verify the join keys exist and are named consistently across tables
names(student_info)
names(student_registration)
names(student_assessment)
names(assessments)
names(courses)
names(student_vle)
names(vle)

# --- Check the key columns match across tables ---
# id_student values should overlap between student_info and student_registration
n_distinct(student_info$id_student)
n_distinct(student_registration$id_student)

# match between id_assessment  studentAssessment and assessments?
n_distinct(student_assessment$id_assessment)
n_distinct(assessments$id_assessment)

# match between student_vle and vle id_site?
n_distinct(student_vle$id_site)
n_distinct(vle$id_site) #96n discrepency means some things werent clicked likely.

# --- Check the composite key is actually unique in student_info ---
# If this number equals nrow(student_info) each combination is unique
student_info |>
  distinct(id_student, code_module, code_presentation) |>
  nrow() #does match

# --- Spot check a few rows of each table ---
head(student_info)
head(student_registration)
head(student_assessment)
head(assessments)
head(courses)
head(student_vle)
head(vle)



# --- Missing value counts per table before joining ---
# Check each raw table independently so we know what missingness
# exists in the source data before any joins are performed.

#--- Takeaway ---
# The join keys themselves are all clean — 
# no missing values in id_student, code_module, code_presentation, id_assessment, 
# or id_site in any table. That's the main thing to verify before joining.
colSums(is.na(student_info))
colSums(is.na(student_registration))
colSums(is.na(student_assessment))
colSums(is.na(assessments))
colSums(is.na(courses))
colSums(is.na(student_vle))
colSums(is.na(vle))









#### Pre-Join Vars ####
#____________________________
#left_join keeps all rows from the left table and attaches
# matching columns from the right. Where no match exists the right columns
# come back as NA — no students accidentally dropped.
#____________________________



# --- Enrich studentAssessment with module context before joining ---
# studentAssessment only has id_assessment and id_student — no module info.
# assessments has the module context (code_module, code_presentation, type,
# due date, weight) linked via id_assessment.
# We join them together first so the enriched table can then join cleanly
# to student_info on all three keys without a many-to-many explosion.
student_assessment_enriched <- student_assessment |>
  left_join(assessments, by = "id_assessment")

glimpse(student_assessment_enriched)






# --- Summarise studentAssessment before joining ---
# Collapses to one row per student per module — keeps the final table at
# student level (32,593 rows). Rows with no recorded score are filtered
# out first to avoid Inf from min/max on all-NA groups. Students with no
# submissions still appear in the final table via the left join as NA.
assessment_summary <- student_assessment_enriched |>
  filter(!is.na(score)) |> #filter() keeps rows where the condition is TRUE
                           #this keeps rows where score is not missing
  group_by(id_student, code_module, code_presentation) |>
  summarise(
    avg_score      = mean(score, na.rm = TRUE),      # average across all assessments
    min_score      = min(score, na.rm = TRUE),       # worst assessment
    max_score      = max(score, na.rm = TRUE),       # best assessment
    n_assessments  = sum(!is.na(score)),             # count of scored assessments
    n_failed       = sum(score < 40, na.rm = TRUE),  # score < 40 = Fail per OULAD docs
    avg_days_early = mean(date - date_submitted,     # submission timing vs due date
                          na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

glimpse(assessment_summary)






# --- Summarise studentVle before joining ---
# studentVle has 10 million rows — joining it raw caused a many-to-many
# explosion and a memory error. Instead we pre-summarize it down to one
# row per student per module (total_clicks and total_days_active) so it
# matches the granularity of the rest of the table.
vle_summary <- student_vle |>
  group_by(id_student, code_module, code_presentation) |>
  summarise(
    total_clicks = sum(sum_click),        # total engagement
    total_days_active = n_distinct(date), # days they logged in
    .groups = "drop"
  )

glimpse(vle_summary)









#### Join ####


# --- Step 1: Start with student demographics ---
# student_info is the central table. Every subsequent join builds outward
# from here.
#Each record is an enrollment. One student can have many enrollments.
oulad_1 <- student_info

# inspect
glimpse(oulad_1)

# --- Step 2: Add registration dates ---
# When the student signed up and (if applicable) when they unregistered.
# Matched on student + module + presentation since the same student can
# take multiple modules and multiple presentations of the same module.
oulad_2 <- oulad_1 |>
  left_join(student_registration,
            by = c("id_student", "code_module", "code_presentation"))

# inspect
glimpse(oulad_2)

# --- Step 3: Add summarised assessment scores ---
# Uses assessment_summary rather than the raw enriched table so the data
# stays at one row per student — 32,593 rows throughout.
# Each student gets a single row of summary stats across all their
# assessments rather than one row per assessment submitted.
oulad_3 <- oulad_2 |>
  left_join(assessment_summary,
            by = c("id_student", "code_module", "code_presentation"))

# inspect
glimpse(oulad_3)

# --- Step 4: Add course/module info ---
# Primarily the length of the module in days from start date to end date.
oulad_4 <- oulad_3 |>
  left_join(courses,
            by = c("code_module", "code_presentation"))

# inspect
glimpse(oulad_4)

# --- Step 5: Add summarised VLE engagement ---
# Joins the pre-summarised VLE table — one row per student per module.
# total_clicks = overall engagement, total_days_active = consistency.
oulad_5 <- oulad_4 |>
  left_join(vle_summary,
            by = c("id_student", "code_module", "code_presentation"))

# inspect
glimpse(oulad_5)


# --- Final dataset ---
oulad <- oulad_5
glimpse(oulad)


# check missingness across all columns
oulad |>
  # for every column count the number of NA values
  # across() applies the function to every column
  # ~ sum(is.na(.)) — for each column, count how many values are NA
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) |>
  glimpse()


#look for patterns of missing visually if helpful.
missmap(oulad)


# --- Add analytical variables ---
# domain groups modules into STEM and Social Sciences based on the
# module codes documented in the OULAD paper.
# wf flags students who did not successfully complete — Withdrawn or Fail
# combined into a single non-completion outcome. D grades don't exist
# in the OU system so DWF reduces to WF here.

#Check these variables we are interested in.
oulad |> distinct(code_module)
oulad |> distinct(final_result)


oulad <- oulad |>
  mutate(
    domain = case_when(
      code_module %in% c("AAA", "BBB", "GGG") ~ "Social Sciences",
      code_module %in% c("CCC", "DDD", "EEE", "FFF") ~ "STEM"
    ),
    wf = case_when(
      final_result %in% c("Withdrawn", "Fail") ~ "WF",
      final_result %in% c("Pass", "Distinction") ~ "Successful"
    )
  )

glimpse(oulad)


# verify domain and wf — any NA means a value wasn't caught by case_when
oulad |> count(domain)
oulad |> count(wf)


#### Load Data ####
# --- Export ---
write_csv(oulad, "oulad_clean.csv")

# --- Import ---
oulad <- read_csv("oulad_clean.csv")
















#### EDA ####
#_______________________________________________________________________________
# EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS
#_______________________________________________________________________________

#_______________________________________________________________________________
# DESCRIPTIVE EDA — WHO ARE OUR STUDENTS?
#_______________________________________________________________________________

# --- Gender ---
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, gender) |>    # one row per unique student
  count(gender) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))


# ---Gender visual starter ---
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, gender) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gender, fill = gender)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(
    title = "Student Body Skews Slightly Male",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )


#________________________________________________________
# vertical adjustment:
# vjust = 1.5    label sits inside the top of the bar
# vjust = 1      label's top edge sits on bar's top edge
# vjust = 0.5    label centered on bar's top edge
# vjust = 0      label's bottom edge sits on bar's top edge
# vjust = -0.5   small gap above the bar
# vjust = -1.5   bigger gap above the bar
#________________________________________________________



# ---Gender hard-coded labels
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, gender) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gender, fill = gender)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "13,739", vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "15,046", vjust = -0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Student Body Skews Slightly Male",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )

# ---Gender hard-coded labels (count and percent)
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, gender) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gender, fill = gender)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "13,739", vjust = -1.6) +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "(47.7%)", vjust = -0.3) +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "15,046", vjust = -1.2) +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "(52.3%)", vjust = 0.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Student Body Skews Slightly Male",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )





# ---Gender hard-coded w. color
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, gender) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gender, fill = gender)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "13,739",  vjust = -1.6) +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "(47.7%)", vjust = -0.3) +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "15,046",  vjust = -1.0) +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "(52.3%)", vjust = .2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("F" = "#aed6f1", "M" = "#3498db")) +
  labs(
    title = "Student Body Skews Slightly Male",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )


# more granular counts
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, gender) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gender, fill = gender)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "13,739",  vjust = -1.6) +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "(47.7%)", vjust = -0.3) +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "15,046",  vjust = -1.0) +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "(52.3%)", vjust =  0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 16000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("F" = "#aed6f1", "M" = "#3498db")) +
  labs(
    title = "Student Body Skews Slightly Male",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )



# bold the labels
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, gender) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gender, fill = gender)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "13,739",  vjust = -1.6, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "(47.7%)", vjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "15,046",  vjust = -1.0, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "(52.3%)", vjust =  0.2, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 16000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("F" = "#aed6f1", "M" = "#3498db")) +
  labs(
    title = "Student Body Skews Slightly Male",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )


# move the counts down on the bars
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, gender) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gender, fill = gender)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "13,739",  vjust =  1.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "(47.7%)", vjust =  3.0, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "15,046",  vjust =  1.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "(52.3%)", vjust =  3.0, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 16000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("F" = "#aed6f1", "M" = "#3498db")) +
  labs(
    title = "Student Body Skews Slightly Male",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )


# remvove the gray background
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, gender) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gender, fill = gender)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "13,739",  vjust =  1.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "F", y = 13739, label = "(47.7%)", vjust =  3.0, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "15,046",  vjust =  1.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "M", y = 15046, label = "(52.3%)", vjust =  3.0, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 16000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("F" = "#aed6f1", "M" = "#3498db")) +
  labs(
    title = "Student Body Skews Slightly Male",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()



# --- Age band ---
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, age_band) |>        # one row per unique student
  count(age_band) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))


# ---Age band visual starter ---
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, age_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age_band, fill = age_band)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(
    title = "Most Students are Under 35",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )



# ---Age band hard-coded labels (counts + percents stacked above)
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, age_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age_band, fill = age_band)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "0-35",  y = 20145, label = "20,145", vjust = -1.6) +
  annotate("text", x = "0-35",  y = 20145, label = "(70.0%)", vjust = -0.3) +
  annotate("text", x = "35-55", y = 8462,  label = "8,462",   vjust = -1.6) +
  annotate("text", x = "35-55", y = 8462,  label = "(29.4%)", vjust = -0.3) +
  annotate("text", x = "55<=",  y = 178,   label = "178",     vjust = -1.6) +
  annotate("text", x = "55<=",  y = 178,   label = "(0.6%)",  vjust = -0.3) +
  labs(
    title = "Most Students are Under 35",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )



# ---Age band hard-coded w. color
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, age_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age_band, fill = age_band)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "0-35",  y = 20145, label = "20,145",  vjust = -1.6) +
  annotate("text", x = "0-35",  y = 20145, label = "(70.0%)", vjust = -0.3) +
  annotate("text", x = "35-55", y = 8462,  label = "8,462",   vjust = -1.6) +
  annotate("text", x = "35-55", y = 8462,  label = "(29.4%)", vjust = -0.3) +
  annotate("text", x = "55<=",  y = 178,   label = "178",     vjust = -1.6) +
  annotate("text", x = "55<=",  y = 178,   label = "(0.6%)",  vjust = -0.3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0-35"  = "#aed6f1",
                               "35-55" = "#3498db",
                               "55<="  = "#1f618d")) +
  labs(
    title = "Most Students are Under 35",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )

  distinct(id_student, age_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age_band, fill = age_band)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "0-35",  y = 20145, label = "20,145",  vjust = -1.6) +

  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 22000, by = 2000)) +

    title = "Most Students are Under 35",

# bold the labels
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, age_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age_band, fill = age_band)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "0-35",  y = 20145, label = "20,145",  vjust = -1.6, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "0-35",  y = 20145, label = "(70.0%)", vjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "35-55", y = 8462,  label = "8,462",   vjust = -1.6, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "35-55", y = 8462,  label = "(29.4%)", vjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "55<=",  y = 178,   label = "178",     vjust = -1.6, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "55<=",  y = 178,   label = "(0.6%)",  vjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 22000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0-35"  = "#aed6f1",
                               "35-55" = "#3498db",
                               "55<="  = "#1f618d")) +
  labs(
    title = "Most Students are Under 35",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )





#________________________________________________________
# vertical adjustment (vjust):
# vjust = 1.5    label sits inside the top of the bar
# vjust = 1      label's top edge sits on bar's top edge
# vjust = 0.5    label centered on bar's top edge
# vjust = 0      label's bottom edge sits on bar's top edge
# vjust = -0.5   small gap above the bar (most common)
# vjust = -1.5   bigger gap above the bar
#________________________________________________________


#________________________________________________________
# horizontal adjustment (hjust):
# hjust = 1.5    label sits well inside the right end of the bar
# hjust = 1      label's right edge sits on bar's right end
# hjust = 0.5    label centered on bar's right end
# hjust = 0      label's left edge sits on bar's right end
# hjust = -0.5   small gap to the right of the bar (most common)
# hjust = -1.5   bigger gap to the right of the bar
#
# heads up: after coord_flip(), hjust controls placement on bar or beside
# the (now horizontal) bar — so use hjust instead of
# vjust for "above/below" the bar tip.
#________________________________________________________


# pass to coord_flip for horizontal bars
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, age_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age_band, fill = age_band)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "0-35",  y = 20145, label = "20,145",  vjust = 0.0, hjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "0-35",  y = 20145, label = "(70.0%)", vjust = 1.5, hjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "35-55", y = 8462,  label = "8,462",   vjust = 0.0, hjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "35-55", y = 8462,  label = "(29.4%)", vjust = 1.5, hjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "55<=",  y = 178,   label = "178",     vjust = 0.0, hjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "55<=",  y = 178,   label = "(0.6%)",  vjust = 1.5, hjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 24000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0-35"  = "#aed6f1",
                                "35-55" = "#3498db",
                                "55<="  = "#1f618d")) +
  labs(
    title = "Most Students are Under 35",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  ) +
  coord_flip()





# pass to coord_flip for horizontal bars — move the labels around
# note: when you 
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, age_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age_band, fill = age_band)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "0-35",  y = 20145, label = "20,145",  vjust = 0.0, hjust =  1.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "0-35",  y = 20145, label = "(70.0%)", vjust = 1.5, hjust =  1.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "35-55", y = 8462,  label = "8,462",   vjust = 0.0, hjust =  1.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "35-55", y = 8462,  label = "(29.4%)", vjust = 1.5, hjust =  1.2, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "55<=",  y = 178,   label = "178",     vjust = 0.0, hjust = -1.2, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "55<=",  y = 178,   label = "(0.6%)",  vjust = 1.5, hjust = -.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 24000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0-35"  = "#aed6f1",
                               "35-55" = "#3498db",
                               "55<="  = "#1f618d")) +
  labs(
    title = "Most Students are Under 35",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()









# --- Prior education --- (ended up not using in proposal so not explored further)
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, highest_education) |>  # one row per unique student
  count(highest_education) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  arrange(desc(n))
#___________________________









# --- IMD band --- 
# excludes the 1,111 students with missing IMD
oulad |>
  filter(!is.na(imd_band)) |>
  distinct(id_student, imd_band) |>        # one row per unique student
  count(imd_band) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))


# ---IMD visual starter ---
oulad |>
  filter(!is.na(imd_band)) |>
  distinct(id_student, imd_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = imd_band, fill = imd_band)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(
    title = "Students Span the Full Socioeconomic Spectrum",
    x = "IMD Band (left = most deprived)",
    y = "Number of students"
  )


# ---Add percent labels above each bar
oulad |>
  filter(!is.na(imd_band)) |>
  distinct(id_student, imd_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = imd_band, fill = imd_band)) +
  geom_bar() +
  annotate("text", x = "0-10%",   y = 2944, label = "10.6%", vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = "10-20",   y = 3132, label = "11.3%", vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = "20-30%",  y = 3200, label = "11.5%", vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = "30-40%",  y = 3128, label = "11.2%", vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = "40-50%",  y = 2890, label = "10.4%", vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = "50-60%",  y = 2787, label = "10.0%", vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = "60-70%",  y = 2560, label = "9.2%",  vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = "70-80%",  y = 2532, label = "9.1%",  vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = "80-90%",  y = 2421, label = "8.7%",  vjust = -0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = "90-100%", y = 2220, label = "8.0%",  vjust = -0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Students Span the Full Socioeconomic Spectrum",
    x = "IMD Band (left = most deprived)",
    y = "Number of students"
  )




#Alternative w. after_stat#
# ---Add percent labels above each bar (computed automatically)
oulad |>
  filter(!is.na(imd_band)) |>            # drop ~1,111 students missing IMD
  distinct(id_student, imd_band) |>       # one row per unique student
  ggplot(aes(x = imd_band, fill = imd_band)) +
  geom_bar() +
  
  # geom_text() places a text label on the chart for every bar.
  # No hardcoded values — ggplot computes the percentages from the data.
  geom_text(
    
    # aes() defines what each label says.
    # after_stat() lets us reach inside ggplot's internal calculations
    # and grab a value (here, "prop" = the proportion of total) that
    # gets calculated during plotting, not from the raw data.
    # scales::percent() formats 0.106 as "10.6%".
    # accuracy = 0.1 keeps one decimal place.
    aes(label = after_stat(scales::percent(prop, accuracy = 0.1)),
        
        # group = 1 tells ggplot to compute proportions across the
        # whole chart, not within each fill group. Without it, each
        # bar would show 100% (since each bar is its own fill group).
        group = 1),
    
    # stat = "count" tells geom_text() to use the same counting that
    # geom_bar() uses internally. This is what makes "count" and "prop"
    # available to after_stat() above.
    stat = "count",
    
    # vjust = -0.5 places the label just above each bar
    # (negative pushes up, positive pushes down)
    vjust = -0.5
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Students Span the Full Socioeconomic Spectrum",
    x     = "IMD Band (left = most deprived)",
    y     = "Number of students"
  )



# ---Clean theme + remove redundant legend + bold labels
oulad |>
  filter(!is.na(imd_band)) |>
  distinct(id_student, imd_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = imd_band, fill = imd_band)) +
  geom_bar(show.legend = FALSE) +
  annotate("text", x = "0-10%",   y = 2944, label = "10.6%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "10-20",   y = 3132, label = "11.3%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "20-30%",  y = 3200, label = "11.5%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "30-40%",  y = 3128, label = "11.2%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "40-50%",  y = 2890, label = "10.4%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "50-60%",  y = 2787, label = "10.0%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "60-70%",  y = 2560, label = "9.2%",  vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "70-80%",  y = 2532, label = "9.1%",  vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "80-90%",  y = 2421, label = "8.7%",  vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "90-100%", y = 2220, label = "8.0%",  vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "RdYlGn") +
  labs(
    title = "Students Span the Full Socioeconomic Spectrum",
    x = "IMD Band (left = most deprived)",
    y = "Number of students"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()



# ---Clean theme + remove redundant legend + bold labels + granular y-axis
oulad |>
  filter(!is.na(imd_band)) |>
  distinct(id_student, imd_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = imd_band, fill = imd_band)) +
  geom_bar(show.legend = FALSE) +
  annotate("text", x = "0-10%",   y = 2944, label = "10.6%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "10-20",   y = 3132, label = "11.3%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "20-30%",  y = 3200, label = "11.5%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "30-40%",  y = 3128, label = "11.2%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "40-50%",  y = 2890, label = "10.4%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "50-60%",  y = 2787, label = "10.0%", vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "60-70%",  y = 2560, label = "9.2%",  vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "70-80%",  y = 2532, label = "9.1%",  vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "80-90%",  y = 2421, label = "8.7%",  vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "90-100%", y = 2220, label = "8.0%",  vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 3500, by = 500)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "RdYlGn") +
  labs(
    title = "Students Span the Full Socioeconomic Spectrum",
    x = "IMD Band (left = most deprived)",
    y = "Number of students"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()




#Alternative where values are saved to a tibble and then used to label with.
# --- IMD band ---
# Step 1: build the summary tibble and save it.
imd_summary <- oulad |>
  filter(!is.na(imd_band)) |>
  distinct(id_student, imd_band) |>
  count(imd_band) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))

# Step 2: build the chart, pointing geom_text() at the summary for its labels.
oulad |>
  filter(!is.na(imd_band)) |>
  distinct(id_student, imd_band) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = imd_band, fill = imd_band)) +
  geom_bar(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(
    data = imd_summary,                              # use the summary, not the main data
    aes(x = imd_band,                                # x position from the summary
        y = n,                                       # y position = bar height
        label = scales::percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)),  # label = formatted percent
    vjust = -0.5,
    fontface = "bold",
    inherit.aes = FALSE                              # ignore the parent ggplot's fill mapping
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 3500, by = 500)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "RdYlGn") +
  labs(
    title = "Students Span the Full Socioeconomic Spectrum",
    x = "IMD Band (left = most deprived)",
    y = "Number of students"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








#____________________________
#Other factors thought about#
# --- Disability ---
oulad |>
  distinct(id_student, disability) |>      # one row per unique student
  count(disability) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))

# --- Previous attempts ---
# how many students are retaking a module?
# note: num_of_prev_attempts is per enrollment not per student
# a student retaking the same module appears multiple times — do NOT distinct here
oulad |>
  count(num_of_prev_attempts) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))


# --- Final result overall ---
# final_result is per enrollment not per student — do NOT distinct here
oulad |>
  count(final_result) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  arrange(desc(n))

# --- Domain split ---
# domain is per enrollment not per student — do NOT distinct here
# we want to know the split of enrollments across domains
# not the number of unique students in each domain
oulad |>
  count(domain) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))




#____________________________
#total
oulad |> count(domain)

oulad |>
  count(domain) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |> #count creates the n, and we divide by the sum of it
  gt() |> #https://gt.rstudio.com/reference/index.html
  cols_label(
    domain = "Domain",
    n      = "Students",
    pct    = "Proportion"
  ) |>
  fmt_percent(columns = pct, decimals = 1) |>
  tab_options(table.align = "left") |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()      # targets the header row
  )


# --- Main numbers ---
# Overall WF rate across the full dataset
oulad |>
  count(wf) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))



# ---WF visual starter ---
oulad |>
  ggplot(aes(x = wf, fill = wf)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(
    title = "More Than Half of All Enrollments End in Withdrawal or Failure",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of enrollments"
  )


# ---Clean theme + remove redundant legend
oulad |>
  ggplot(aes(x = wf, fill = wf)) +
  geom_bar(show.legend = FALSE) +
  annotate("text", x = "Successful", y = 15385, label = "15,385",  vjust = 2.6, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "Successful", y = 15385, label = "(47.2%)", vjust = 1.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "WF",         y = 17208, label = "17,208",  vjust = 2.6, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "WF",         y = 17208, label = "(52.8%)", vjust = 1.3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 18000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Successful" = "#7dcea0",
                               "WF"         = "#d98880")) +
  labs(
    title = "More Than Half of All Enrollments End in Withdrawal or Failure",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of enrollments"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()




#Alternative - percentages on the y:
# ---Y axis as percentages, values typed directly from the values we got before above
tibble(
  wf  = c("Successful", "WF"),
  n   = c(15385, 17208),
  pct = c(0.472, 0.528)
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = wf, y = pct, fill = wf)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  annotate("text", x = "Successful", y = 0.472, label = "15,385",  vjust = 2.6, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "Successful", y = 0.472, label = "(47.2%)", vjust = 1.3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "WF",         y = 0.528, label = "17,208",  vjust = 2.6, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = "WF",         y = 0.528, label = "(52.8%)", vjust = 1.3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, breaks = seq(0, 0.6, by = 0.1)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Successful" = "#7dcea0",
                               "WF"         = "#d98880")) +
  labs(
    title = "More Than Half of All Enrollments End in Withdrawal or Failure",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Percent of enrollments"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()




#____________________________
#alternative to a manual entry
# Step 1 — precompute the summary
wf_summary <- oulad |>
  count(wf) |>                                      # one row per outcome
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))                          # add proportion column

wf_summary    # check the values


# Step 2 — pass the summary to ggplot
wf_summary |>
  ggplot(aes(x = wf, y = pct, fill = wf)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)), #use the number as the label
            vjust = 2.6, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)), #use the pct as the label
            vjust = 1.3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent,
                     breaks = seq(0, 0.6, by = 0.1)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Successful" = "#7dcea0",
                               "WF"         = "#d98880")) +
  labs(
    title = "More Than Half of All Enrollments End in Withdrawal or Failure",
    x = NULL, y = "Percent of enrollments"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()






# WF rate by domain — a core question
oulad |>
  group_by(domain) |>
  summarise(
    n_students = n(),
    n_wf       = sum(wf == "WF", na.rm = TRUE),
    wf_rate    = mean(wf == "WF", na.rm = TRUE)
  )


# --- WF rate vis ---
oulad |>
  group_by(domain) |>
  summarise(wf_rate = mean(wf == "WF", na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = domain, y = wf_rate, fill = domain)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "STEM Modules Carry a Higher WF Rate Than Social Sciences",
    x = NULL,
    y = "WF Rate"
  )

#Add counts/percents
# Step 1 — precompute the summary
domain_summary <- oulad |>
  group_by(domain) |>
  summarise(
    n_wf    = sum(wf == "WF", na.rm = TRUE),
    wf_rate = mean(wf == "WF", na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

domain_summary    # check the values before plotting


# Step 2 — pass the summary to ggplot
domain_summary |>
  ggplot(aes(x = domain, y = wf_rate, fill = domain)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n_wf)),
            vjust = -1.6, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(wf_rate, accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(
    title = "STEM Modules Carry a Higher WF Rate Than Social Sciences",
    x = NULL,
    y = "WF Rate"
  )






#_______________________________________________________________________________

#_______________________________________________________________________________
# --- Visualize WF rate by domain ---
# WF by domain — annotated, dodged, counts on y


oulad |>
  ggplot(aes(x = domain, fill = wf)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", width = 0.9) +
  # STEM (x = 2): Successful bar offset left, WF bar offset right
  annotate("text", x = 1.775, y = 9586,  label = "9,586",   vjust = 2.9, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 1.775, y = 9586,  label = "(44.8%)", vjust = 1.2, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 2.225, y = 11816, label = "11,816",  vjust = 2.9, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 2.225, y = 11816, label = "(55.2%)", vjust = 1.2, fontface = "bold") +
  # Social Sciences (x = 1): Successful bar offset left, WF bar offset right
  annotate("text", x = 0.775, y = 5799,  label = "5,799",   vjust = 2.9, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.775, y = 5799,  label = "(51.8%)", vjust = 1.2, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 1.225, y = 5392,  label = "5,392",   vjust = 2.9, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 1.225, y = 5392,  label = "(48.2%)", vjust = 1.2, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 13000, by = 2000)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Successful" = "#7dcea0",
    "WF"         = "#d98880"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "STEM Modules Carry a Higher WF Rate Than Social Sciences",
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "Number of students",
    fill  = "Outcome"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()




#alternative with facet wrap
# Step 1 — precompute counts and percents per domain × outcome
domain_outcomes <- oulad |>
  count(domain, wf) |>                              # 4 rows: STEM/WF, STEM/Successful, SS/WF, SS/Successful
  group_by(domain) |>                               # group by domain so percents are within domain
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>                       # add proportion column (sums to 100% per domain)
  ungroup()                                         # drop the grouping for cleaner downstream use

domain_outcomes    # check the values before plotting


# Step 2 — pass the summary to ggplot
domain_outcomes |>
  ggplot(aes(x = wf, y = n, fill = wf)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)), #add count
            vjust = 2.3, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(pct, accuracy = 1)), #add percent
            vjust = 1.2, fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~ domain) +
  labs(
    title = "STEM Modules Carry a Higher WF Rate Than Social Sciences",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  )



# Step 3 — finish styling the plot
domain_outcomes |>
  ggplot(aes(x = wf, y = n, fill = wf)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)),
            vjust = 2.3, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(pct, accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = 1.2, fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~ domain) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Successful" = "#7dcea0",
    "WF"         = "#d98880"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "STEM Modules Carry a Higher WF Rate Than Social Sciences",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of students"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()












#_______________________________________________________________________________
# --- Break it down further ---

# WF rate by individual module
# reveals whether the gap is driven by one outlier module or consistent

# Step 1 — precompute WF rate per module
module_wf <- oulad |>
  group_by(domain, code_module) |>
  summarise(
    wf_rate = mean(wf == "WF", na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

module_wf    # check the values before plotting


# Step 2 — pass the summary to ggplot
module_wf |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(code_module, wf_rate),
             y = wf_rate,
             fill = domain)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(wf_rate, accuracy = 1)),
            hjust = -0.2, fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "STEM Modules Show Higher WF Rates, BBB is the Notable Exception",
    x = NULL,
    y = "WF Rate"
  )



#style
module_wf |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(code_module, wf_rate),
             y = wf_rate,
             fill = domain)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(wf_rate, accuracy = 1)),
            hjust = -0.2, fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "STEM"            = "#3498db",
    "Social Sciences" = "#aed6f1"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "STEM Modules Show Higher WF Rates, BBB is the Notable Exception",
    x = NULL,
    y = "WF Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#_______________________________________________________________________________
# --- Step 4: Demographics and wf ---

# WF rate by gender
# count(gender, wf) creates one row per gender/outcome combination
# group_by(gender) means sum(n) will total within each gender
# mutate(pct) calculates the proportion of each outcome within each gender
# filter(wf == "WF") keeps only the WF rows — we just want the failure rate
# Step 1 — precompute counts and WF rate per gender
gender_wf <- oulad |>
  count(gender, wf) |>                              # one row per gender/outcome combo
  group_by(gender) |>                               # group so percents calc within each gender
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>                       # proportion within gender (sums to 100% per gender)
  filter(wf == "WF") |>                             # keep only WF rows — one row per gender
  ungroup()                                         # drop grouping for cleaner downstream use

gender_wf    # check the values before plotting


# Step 2 — barebones chart
gender_wf |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gender, y = pct, fill = gender)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "WF Rates are Slightly Higher for Males",
    x = NULL,
    y = "WF Rate"
  )


# Step 3 — finished chart, styled
gender_wf |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gender, y = pct, fill = gender)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(pct, accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -1.6, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)),
            vjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 0.65)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "M" = "#3498db",
    "F" = "#aed6f1"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "WF Rates are Slightly Higher for Males",
    x = NULL,
    y = "WF Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()





# WF rate by age band
# count(age_band, wf) creates one row per age band/outcome combination
# group_by(age_band) means pct is calculated within each age band
# filter(wf == "WF") keeps only the WF rows — one bar per age band
# Step 1 — precompute counts and WF rate per age band
age_wf <- oulad |>
  count(age_band, wf) |>                            # one row per age band/outcome combo
  group_by(age_band) |>                             # group so percents calc within each age band
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>                       # proportion within age band (sums to 100% per band)
  filter(wf == "WF") |>                             # keep only WF rows — one row per age band
  ungroup()                                         # drop grouping for cleaner downstream use

age_wf    # check the values before plotting


# Step 2 — barebones chart
age_wf |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age_band, y = pct, fill = age_band)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "WF Rates Decrease with Age",
    x = NULL,
    y = "WF Rate"
  )


# Step 3 — finished chart, styled
age_wf |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age_band, y = pct, fill = age_band)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(pct, accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -1.6, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)),
            vjust = -0.3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 0.75)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues") +
  labs(
    title = "WF Rates Decrease with Age",
    x = NULL,
    y = "WF Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()







# WF rate by IMD band (didnt use)
# IMD band is ordered from most deprived (0-10%) to least deprived (90-100%)
oulad |>
  filter(!is.na(imd_band)) |>               # exclude the 1,111 missing IMD records
  count(imd_band, wf) |>
  group_by(imd_band) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  filter(wf == "WF") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = imd_band, y = pct, fill = imd_band)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "RdYlGn") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(
    title = "WF Rates are Higher in Deprived IMD Band",
    subtitle = "Lower IMD band means more socioeconomic deprivation",
    x     = "IMD Band",
    y     = "WF Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))







#imd band line
# Step 1 — precompute WF rate per IMD band
imd_wf <- oulad |>
  filter(!is.na(imd_band)) |>                       # exclude 1,111 students missing IMD data
  count(imd_band, wf) |>                            # one row per IMD band/outcome combo
  group_by(imd_band) |>                             # group so percents calc within each band
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>                       # proportion within band
  filter(wf == "WF") |>                             # keep only WF rows — one row per band
  ungroup()                                         # drop grouping

imd_wf    # check the values before plotting


# Step 2 — barebones chart
imd_wf |>
  ggplot(aes(x = imd_band, y = pct, group = 1)) +   # group = 1 connects all points with one line
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "WF Rates are Higher in Deprived IMD Bands",
    x = "IMD Band",
    y = "WF Rate"
  )


# Step 3 — finished chart, styled
imd_wf |>
  ggplot(aes(x = imd_band, y = pct, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(color = "#3498db", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(color = "#3498db", size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(pct, accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -1, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0.3, 0.75)) +
  labs(
    title    = "WF Rates are Higher in Deprived IMD Bands",
    subtitle = "Lower IMD bands reflect more SES deprivation",
    x        = "IMD Band",
    y        = "WF Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))






# WF rate by prior education (#didnt use)
oulad |>
  count(highest_education, wf) |>
  group_by(highest_education) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  filter(wf == "WF") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(highest_education, pct), y = pct, fill = highest_education)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "WF Rate by Prior Education",
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "WF Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

#_______________________________________________________________________________
# ---  VLE engagement by outcome ---
# Do WF students engage less with course materials?
# scatterplot — VLE engagement vs assessment score
# question: do students who engage more with course materials score higher?


#unscaled
# without log scale — most points pile up on the left, outliers stretch axis right
oulad |>
  filter(!is.na(avg_score), !is.na(total_clicks)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = total_clicks, y = avg_score, color = wf)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3)



# --- Why log scale on x? ---
# total_clicks ranges from 1 to ~50,000 across students
# a small number of very high-engagement students create extreme outliers
# on a linear scale those outliers stretch the x axis so far right that
# the majority of students get compressed into an unreadable pile on the left
# scale_x_log10() compresses the axis so each step is a multiplication by 10
# rather than a fixed addition — 1, 10, 100, 1000, 10000
# this spreads the points evenly and makes the relationship visible



# How to read it — concrete example:
#  logged in to the VLE maybe
# once or twice over the term. They opened the course page, clicked
# around briefly, and largely disappeared. A student at the 100-click
# mark engaged TEN TIMES more than a student sitting at the 10-click mark — 
# They are not "a little more active" than the 10-click student; they are an order of magnitude
# more active. The visual distance between 10 and 100 looks the same
# as the distance between 100 and 1,000, and between 1,000 and 10,000
# — every equal step on the axis means another 10x increase in
# engagement. So a student at 1,000 clicks is 100 times more active
# than the 10-click student, even though they only look "three ticks
# apart" on the chart.




oulad |>
  filter(
    !is.na(avg_score),      # exclude students with no recorded assessment scores
    !is.na(total_clicks)    # exclude students with no VLE interactions
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x     = total_clicks,   # VLE engagement on x axis
    y     = avg_score,      # assessment performance on y axis
    color = wf              # color points by outcome — WF vs Successful
  )) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) + # alpha = 0.3 makes points semi-transparent
  # essential with 32k points — without it everything
  # overlaps into a solid unreadable mass
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +  # log scale — see rationale above
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "WF"         = "#d98880",  
    "Successful" = "#7dcea0"   
  )) +
  labs(
    title   = "Successful Students Engage More and Score Higher",
    subtitle = "WF students cluster at low engagement",
    x       = "Total VLE Clicks (log scale)",
    y       = "Average Assessment Score",
    color   = "Outcome"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#_______________________________________________________________________________
#summary of findings table

# Step 1 — build the summary tibble with columns
findings_summary <- tibble(
  group = c( #column 1
    "Overall", 
    "Overall",
    "Point 1 — Domain Gap", 
    "Point 1 — Domain Gap",
    "Point 2 — SES Background (IMD Band)", 
    "Point 2 — SES Background (IMD Band)",
    "Point 2 — Age Band", 
    "Point 2 — Age Band",
    "Point 2 — Gender", 
    "Point 2 — Gender",
    "Point 3 — VLE Engagement (Median VLE Clicks)", 
    "Point 3 — VLE Engagement (Median VLE Clicks)"
  ),
  finding = c( #column 2
    "Overall WF Rate", #overall group
    "Overall Successful Rate",
    "STEM", #domain group
    "Social Sciences",
    "Most Deprived (0-10%)", #age group
    "Least Deprived (90-100%)",
    "Age 0-35", 
    "Age 55+",
    "Male", #gender
    "Female",
    "Successful Students",#vle 
    "WF Students"
  ),
  rate = c(
    "53%", "47%",
    "55%", "48%",
    "65%", "42%",
    "55%", "38%",
    "54%", "52%",
    "1,425 clicks", "280 clicks"
  ),
  gap = c(
    "6%", "",
    "7%", "",
    "23%", "",
    "17%", "",
    "2%", "",
    "80%", ""
  )
)

findings_summary    # check the values to see


# Step 2 — pass the summary to gt with a single groupname_col argument
findings_summary |>
  gt(groupname_col = "group") |>
  cols_label(
    finding = "Metric", #new column names for the resulting table
    rate    = "Rate",
    gap     = "Gap"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#d6eaf8"),
    locations = cells_row_groups()
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_body(columns = gap)
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    table.align                     = "left",
    row.striping.include_table_body = FALSE, #prevent qmd default styling
    quarto.disable_processing       = TRUE
  )






#budget table

# Step 1 — build the allocation summary tibble with a group column
allocation_summary <- tibble(
  group = c( #column 1
    "By Domain", 
    "By Domain",
    "By IMD Band", 
    "By IMD Band", 
    "By IMD Band",
    "By Age Band", 
    "By Age Band", 
    "By Age Band"
  ),
  students = c(
    "STEM", 
    "Social Sciences",
    "IMD 0-30% (Most Deprived)", 
    "IMD 30-70% (Middle)", 
    "IMD 70-100% (Least Deprived)",
    "Age 0-35", 
    "Age 35-55", 
    "Age 55+"
  ),
  wf_rate             = c(0.55, 0.48, 0.62, 0.52, 0.44, 0.55, 0.48, 0.38),
  enrollments         = c(21402, 11191, 9200, 12800, 9400, 16500, 12800, 3293),
  current_per_enroll  = c(84, 107, 90, 92, 94, 88, 96, 104),
  proposed_per_enroll = c(120, 85, 135, 95, 75, 115, 90, 80)
) |>
  mutate(
    current_total  = enrollments * current_per_enroll,
    proposed_total = enrollments * proposed_per_enroll,
    gap            = proposed_total - current_total
  )

allocation_summary    # check the values 


# Step 2 — pass the summary to gt
allocation_summary |>
  gt(groupname_col = "group") |>                        # use the section column for row groups
  cols_label(
    students            = "Students",
    wf_rate             = "WF Rate",
    enrollments         = "Enrollments",
    current_per_enroll  = "Current $/Enrollment",
    proposed_per_enroll = "Proposed $/Enrollment",
    current_total       = "Current Total",
    proposed_total      = "Proposed Total",
    gap                 = "Funding Gap"
  ) |>
  fmt_percent(columns = wf_rate, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_number(columns = enrollments, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_currency(
    columns    = c(current_per_enroll, proposed_per_enroll,
                   current_total, proposed_total, gap),
    currency   = "USD",
    decimals   = 0,
    accounting = TRUE
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Current vs Proposed Resource Allocation by Risk Group",
    subtitle = "Proposed allocation weighted by WF rate — higher risk groups receive more per enrollment"
  ) |>
  grand_summary_rows(
    columns = c(current_total, proposed_total, gap),
    fns     = list(`Total Net Ask` = ~ sum(.[gap > 0], na.rm = TRUE)),
    fmt     = ~ fmt_currency(., currency = "USD", decimals = 0, accounting = TRUE)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#7dcea0"),
    locations = cells_body(columns = gap, rows = gap > 0)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#d98880"),
    locations = cells_body(columns = gap, rows = gap < 0)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#d6eaf8"),
    locations = cells_row_groups()
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    table.align                     = "left",
    row.striping.include_table_body = FALSE,
    quarto.disable_processing       = TRUE,
    column_labels.font.weight       = "bold"          # the simpler bold-headers shortcut
  )

8.1 Additional Tables or Figures

Any supplementary material that supports the analysis but would interrupt the flow of the main document goes here.
